claims.claim: We investigate whether transformer feed-forward network (FFN) layers can be thinned using masks conditioned solely on the current token identity, preserving model quality more effectively than global or random thinning at equivalent retained widths.

evidence_refs.quote: # Token-Conditioned MLP Thinning: Per-Token Sparse Masks for Feed-Forward Network Compression

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: On a controlled synthetic next-token prediction task with a small causal transformer (2 layers, *d*=96, *d*<sub>ff</sub>=384), we calibrate per-token FFN activation magnitudes and construct three equal-budget mask strategies: token-conditioned top-*k*, global top-*k*, and random per-token.

evidence_refs.quote: # Token-Conditioned MLP Thinning: Per-Token Sparse Masks for Feed-Forward Network Compression

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Across three random seeds at 25% retained FFN width, token-conditioned masks yield a mean loss increase of 0.0146 over the dense baseline, compared to 0.1101 for global top-*k* and 0.1378 for random per-token masks—approximately 7.5× and 9.4× lower degradation, respectively.

evidence_refs.quote: # Token-Conditioned MLP Thinning: Per-Token Sparse Masks for Feed-Forward Network Compression

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: At 12.5% retained width, the advantage narrows but remains approximately 4.5× and 5.0×.

claims.claim: We ask a simpler question: if the only conditioning signal is the current token id—available at no additional compute cost—can we construct per-token FFN masks that preserve quality substantially better than global or random masks at the same sparsity budget?
